issues.summary: ExplorationHandlers retains two dead private response-building methods (buildBaseResponseEnvelope, buildMultiSeedResponseEnvelope) that were not removed when response assembly was consolidated into ExploreResponseAssembler
issues.suggestion: Delete buildBaseResponseEnvelope, buildMultiSeedResponseEnvelope, and the private BaseEnvelope record from ExplorationHandlers. Response assembly responsibility is already wholly owned by ExploreResponseAssembler; removing these orphaned helpers eliminates the split-boundary confusion.
evidence: buildBaseResponseEnvelope(String, SharedExploreParams) at line 228 of ExplorationHandlers builds a Map<String,Object> envelope — the old pre-typed pattern
buildMultiSeedResponseEnvelope(String, SharedExploreParams, List<?>) at line 215 calls buildBaseResponseEnvelope but is itself never called by any handler method

The handlers handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti and handleSemanticFieldComparison both delegate to ExploreResponseAssembler typed-record builders, meaning the Map-based envelope helpers are fully orphaned
The BaseEnvelope record (line 206) exists solely to support these dead helpers and is also unreachable from any handler

issues.summary: api.ExplorationHandlers constructs domain option objects (ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions) from raw HTTP parameters, placing domain model instantiation inside the HTTP layer
issues.suggestion: This is a moderate coupling issue rather than a hard boundary violation; the option types are pure value objects with no behaviour. A pragmatic improvement would be to add static factory methods on each option type (e.g. ExplorationOptions.fromHttpParams(int top, double minLogDice, int minShared)) so that parsing-to-construction logic is co-located with the type. Alternatively, if the SPI is refactored to accept a relation id string (see spi_leaks_relation_config), the HTTP parameter surface could be encapsulated in a dedicated request object to further decouple the handler from domain constructors.
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExplore constructs new SingleSeedExplorationOptions(new ExplorationOptions(top, minLogDice, minShared), nounsPerSeed) directly from parsed HTTP params before calling semanticFieldExplorer.exploreByRelation()
handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti and handleSemanticFieldComparison each construct an ExplorationOptions from HTTP params
handleSemanticFieldExamples constructs new FetchExamplesOptions(top) inline
This means adding a new option field to ExplorationOptions forces a change in the HTTP handler as well as the domain model — the api layer is the de-facto constructor for domain option types

issues.summary: RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES statically hardcodes grammar-config relation IDs, creating silent drift risk when IDs change
issues.suggestion: Validate RELATION_ALIASES entries against the loaded GrammarConfig at server startup: after loading grammarConfig, iterate RELATION_ALIASES.values() and assert each value matches a known relation ID (grammarConfig.relation(id).isPresent()), throwing IllegalStateException on mismatch. This converts silent runtime drift into a fast startup failure. Alternatively, load aliases directly from the grammar config JSON (e.g. an 'aliases' field per relation), eliminating the static map entirely.
evidence: RELATION_ALIASES = Map.of("adj_modifier", "noun_modifiers", "adj_predicate", "noun_adj_predicates", ...) in RelationUtils — eight hardcoded grammar-config ID strings.

GrammarConfig holds the authoritative list of relation IDs parsed from relations.json at runtime; RelationUtils has no reference to GrammarConfig and cannot validate its hardcoded aliases against the loaded config.

If a grammar config relation ID is renamed (e.g. 'noun_modifiers' → 'adj_modifiers'), RelationUtils.resolveRelationAlias silently returns the stale ID; the API call will find no matching relation and return empty results with no error.

issues.summary: API handler tests inject empty stubs and only assert response structure, leaving semantic correctness of populated JSON responses untested
issues.suggestion: Add at least one parametric 'data-shape' test per handler that injects a stub returning known non-empty data and asserts specific values in the JSON output (e.g. first collocate name, logDice value, edge source/target). The compare_edgeWeights_abstractHasCorrectWeightToTheory test in ExplorationHandlersTest is the right template — replicate it for SketchHandlers and ConcordanceHandlers.
evidence: ExplorationHandlersTest.handleSemanticFieldExplore_validRequest_returns200 asserts status=ok and edges key presence, but the stub returns empty collocates so the edges array is always [] — correctness of populated edge payloads is never verified

SketchHandlersTest.handleSketchRequest_fullSketch_returns200WithRelationsMap verifies that a 'relations' key exists but does not assert collocate names, logDice values or ordering in the relations map
The one test that does verify semantic content is compare_edgeWeights_abstractHasCorrectWeightToTheory in ExplorationHandlersTest — this pattern is not applied consistently across handlers

issues.summary: Several tests construct RelationConfig via positional constructor — a brittle pattern that silently passes wrong data if parameter order changes
issues.suggestion: Replace inline RelationConfig constructor calls in exploration tests with GrammarConfigHelper.requireTestConfig().relations().stream().findFirst().orElseThrow(), matching the pattern already used in MultiSeedExplorerTest. This eliminates the positional-arg coupling and keeps tests aligned with the grammar fixture.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorerTest.exploreMultiSeed_returnsNonNullResult calls new RelationConfig("test", "test", "test", "[xpos=\"NN.*\"] [xpos=\"JJ.*\"]", 1, 2, false, 0, RelationType.SURFACE, PosGroup.ADJ) — 10 positional args with no field names
SemanticFieldExplorerInternalTest.adjRelationConfig() duplicates the same 10-arg inline construction
GrammarConfigHelper.requireTestConfig() already provides a canonical test RelationConfig and is used in MultiSeedExplorerTest.anyRelation() — the inline constructions are unnecessary

issues.summary: Deprecated Map-based assembly methods (populateExploreResponse, populateComparisonResponse) coexist with typed-record builders and are still exercised by tests
issues.suggestion: Migrate the test assertions to use the typed-record builders (buildSingleSeedExploreResponse etc.) and delete the deprecated Map-based methods and their formatSeedCollocates/formatDiscoveredNouns/formatCoreCollocates helpers once tests pass. This collapses the two paths into one.
evidence: populateExploreResponse and populateComparisonResponse are marked @Deprecated but are called directly in ExploreResponseAssemblerTest (lines 84, 101), keeping both code paths live.

The typed-record path (buildSingleSeedExploreResponse, buildMultiSeedExploreResponse, buildComparisonResponse) is what production handlers use, but the Map-based path has no clear removal timeline and its test coverage creates an ongoing maintenance obligation.

issues.summary: ExploreResponse encodes two mutually exclusive JSON shapes (single-seed vs multi-seed) in one record via nullable discriminator fields
issues.suggestion: Extract two subtypes: SingleSeedExploreResponse and MultiSeedExploreResponse, sharing a common base interface or sealed parent. Alternatively, add compact constructor validation (Objects.requireNonNull logic) that throws if neither seed nor seeds is present. Either approach makes the two-shape contract explicit at the type boundary.
evidence: ExploreResponse Javadoc states: 'The seed field is set for single-seed responses and null for multi-seed; the seeds and seedCount fields are set for multi-seed responses and null for single-seed.'

No sealed interface, factory method, or compile-time check prevents constructing an ExploreResponse with seed=null AND seeds=null, which would produce a structurally invalid response silently accepted by Jackson.

issues.summary: buildExamplesResponse has 7 parameters including an awkward @Nullable Boolean fallback
issues.suggestion: Introduce an ExamplesRequest or ExamplesContext value object bundling seed, collocate, relation, bcql, top, and a boolean fallback (non-nullable, defaulting to false). The builder then takes (ExamplesContext ctx, List<CollocateResult> results). Replace 'fallback ? true : null' with a plain boolean at the call site.
evidence: Signature: static ExamplesResponse buildExamplesResponse(@NonNull String seed, @NonNull String collocate, @NonNull String relation, @NonNull String bcql, int top, @Nullable Boolean fallback, @NonNull List<CollocateResult> results) — seven parameters.
@Nullable Boolean fallback is a three-valued nullable boolean; callers pass 'fallback ? true : null' (ConcordanceHandlers line ~65), which is awkward and error-prone.

issues.summary: buildComparisonResponse takes three bare scalar parameters (top, minShared, minLogDice) that are already bundled as SharedExploreParams inside ExplorationHandlers
issues.suggestion: Promote SharedExploreParams to package-visible (or move it to the model package) and use it as a single parameter in buildComparisonResponse, buildSingleSeedExploreResponse, and buildMultiSeedExploreResponse. This closes the gap between the record that already exists and the flat parameter list that the assembler exposes.
evidence: buildComparisonResponse(List<String> seeds, String relationType, int top, int minShared, double minLogDice, ComparisonResult result) — three consecutive scalars with no parameter object.

ExplorationHandlers already defines 'private record SharedExploreParams(int topCollocates, int minShared, double minLogDice)' but it is private to the handler and not passed to the assembler, forcing the caller to destructure it: buildComparisonResponse(..., commonParams.topCollocates(), commonParams.minShared(), commonParams.minLogDice()).

issues.summary: buildMultiSeedResponseEnvelope and buildBaseResponseEnvelope are private methods in ExplorationHandlers that are never called
issues.suggestion: Delete both methods and the BaseEnvelope record. They are unreachable and represent leftover scaffolding from the Map-based response era.
evidence: buildMultiSeedResponseEnvelope is defined at line 215 but grep finds no call site outside its own definition; buildBaseResponseEnvelope is only called by buildMultiSeedResponseEnvelope.
All three handlers now delegate to typed ExploreResponseAssembler methods, leaving these Map-based envelope builders orphaned.

issues.summary: Wildcard CORS origin is only warned about at startup, not prevented; any operator misconfiguration silently opens all origins
issues.suggestion: In warnIfWildcardCors(), throw an IllegalStateException (or at minimum log at ERROR level) when the system property is '*' and the server is not in an explicitly acknowledged dev mode. Alternatively, document wildcard as an accepted risk with an explicit --allow-wildcard-cors flag so it is a conscious opt-in.
evidence: warnIfWildcardCors() calls logger.warn(...) when cors.allow.origin is '*' but returns normally, allowing the server to start with unrestricted CORS.
The class Javadoc says any bind-address change requires an auth layer, but does not say that wildcard CORS is equally dangerous when the bind address is changed.
